lks2.objective: Practise stepping up as a captain — communicating clearly, encouraging teammates and helping the team make good decisions in a fast-moving tag game.
successCriteria: Captains take ownership of a 30-second team talk

uks2.objective: Lead a team with tactical thinking — assign defenders/attackers, debrief between rounds, adjust strategy based on what's working.
successCriteria: Captain assigns roles before each round
Team talks identify a concrete change
Players respect captain decisions without ego

space.description: Square area sized to your group. Split children into three smaller teams: Blues, Yellows and Non-Bibs.

value.name: Responsibility
value.text: Responsibility means stepping up, helping others and taking ownership of your role. Being captain isn't about being the best player — it's about communicating clearly, encouraging teammates and helping the team make good decisions. Everyone gets a turn at it.

steps: Split into three teams: Blues, Yellows, Non-Bibs. All teams play at the same time.
Each team picks a captain for the round.
Aim: tag players from the OTHER teams while protecting your own teammates.
If tagged, freeze on the spot. Only a teammate from your OWN team can free you.
Captain's job: communicate tactics at the start, encourage during play, help free teammates quickly.
After each round (~2 min), stop and give captains 30–60 sec to lead a quick team talk.
Rotate captains every round so different children experience leadership.
progressions: Introduce footballs — all players dribble while tagging and avoiding tags.
Tagged players must complete three toe taps before a teammate can free them.
questions: What did your captain do well?
What's one thing you'd change about your strategy next round?
How can a captain make teammates feel supported?

step.space: Reduce area size to increase agility, scanning and quick reactions
step.task: Change the re-entry challenge — keepy-ups or a specific turn before being freed
step.equipment: Introduce small home zones where children can briefly rest before rejoining
step.people: Adjust team sizes — make one team smaller to challenge stronger players

base.showmanship: Use the breaks to support captains quietly. Move between teams with prompts like "What could you change this round?" or "How can you free teammates quicker?" — show parents that you're coaching leadership, not just running a tag game.
